title: Like a list, but locked
instruction:
You know lists — flexible, changeable, great for collections you build up over time.

But some data should **never change**. GPS coordinates. RGB colour values. Database row IDs. Days of the week.

For that, Python has tuples. A tuple is like a list, but once you create it, it's locked forever.

Use parentheses instead of square brackets:

`point = (10, 20)` instead of `point = [10, 20]`

Run this.
starter:
coordinates = (40.7128, -74.0060)  # New York City
print(coordinates)
print(type(coordinates))
print(f"Latitude: {coordinates[0]}")
print(f"Longitude: {coordinates[1]}")
successMessage: A tuple. Looks like a list with parentheses. Reads like a list. But try to change it and Python says no. That's the whole point — coordinates should never change.

title: Try to change it — it won't work
instruction:
Tuples are immutable. Trying to change them causes a `TypeError` — Python refuses.

This is NOT a bug. This IS the feature. The error protects you.

Run this to see the error. Then comment out the broken line so the code runs cleanly.
hint: Put # in front of the point[0] = 999 line.
starter:
point = (10, 20)
print(point)
# This will crash:
point[0] = 999  # BUG: tuples are immutable — comment this out
print(point)

codeChecks.message: Comment out the line that tries to modify the tuple.

successMessage: TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment. Python is protecting you from accidentally changing data that should be permanent. Immutability is a feature, not a limitation.

title: Unpacking — unbox the values
instruction:
Tuples support **unpacking** — assign each value to a separate variable in one line:

```python
point = (10, 20)
x, y = point  # x gets 10, y gets 20
```

Unpack the RGB colour tuple into three separate variables.
starter:
color = (255, 128, 0)  # Orange
# TODO: unpack into r, g, b variables
print(f"Red: {r}, Green: {g}, Blue: {b}")

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: r=255, g=128, b=0. Unpacking is much cleaner than color[0], color[1], color[2]. And by the way — this is exactly the same swap trick you learned in Chapter 2: first, last = last, first

title: The single-element trap
instruction:
Quick quiz: is `(5)` a tuple?

Nope. Python treats `(5)` as just `5` in parentheses — like math: `(2 + 3)`.

To make a single-element tuple, you need a **trailing comma**:

`(5,)` — that's a tuple with one element.

Run this to see the difference.
starter:
not_a_tuple = (5)
actual_tuple = (5,)

print(type(not_a_tuple))
print(type(actual_tuple))
print(not_a_tuple)
print(actual_tuple)
successMessage: The trailing comma makes it a tuple. Without it, Python just sees parentheses around a number. This trips up everyone once. Now you know before it bites you.

title: List vs Tuple — when to use which
instruction:
Use a **list** when:
- You'll add, remove, or change items
- You're building up a collection

Use a **tuple** when:
- The data is fixed and should never change
- You want protection against accidental modification
- You need to use it as a dictionary key (lists can't be dict keys!)

Fix the code: coordinates should be a tuple (they're fixed), names should be a list (they can change).
starter:
# BUG: coordinates should be a tuple (fixed data)
coordinates = [40.7128, -74.0060]

# BUG: names should be a list (may change)
names = ("Alice", "Bob", "Carol")

print(type(coordinates))
print(type(names))
successMessage: Right tool for the right job. Tuple for fixed data, list for flexible data. Simple rule that saves you from many bugs.

title: Tuples as dictionary keys
instruction:
Here's a superpower tuples have that lists don't: **you can use tuples as dictionary keys**.

Why? Because dictionary keys need to be immutable (unchanging). Tuples are. Lists aren't.

This lets you map coordinate pairs, date ranges, or any compound value to something.

Create a dictionary that maps location tuples to city names. Then look one up.
starter:
# TODO: create a dict with tuple keys mapping coordinates to city names
# Include at least 2 cities, then look one up by its tuple key

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Tuple as a dictionary key! This unlocks multi-dimensional lookups: map (row, column) to a chess piece, (year, month) to monthly revenue, (lat, lng) to a location name.

title: namedtuple — readable tuple fields
instruction:
`collections.namedtuple` makes tuples even better — you can access fields by name instead of position.

```python
Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y'])
p = Point(10, 20)
print(p.x)   # 10 — way cleaner than p[0]
```

Create a `Person` namedtuple with `name`, `age`, and `city` fields.
starter:
from collections import namedtuple
# TODO: create Person namedtuple with name, age, city fields
# Then create a Person and print name, age, and city using dot notation

codeChecks.message: Access the name field with dot notation: person.name
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Named fields on an immutable tuple. person.name is so much clearer than person[0]. This is the predecessor to dataclasses (Chapter 17) — same idea, older approach.

title: Build a coordinate mapper
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a coordinate mapper:
1. Create a list of at least 3 city tuples: `(name, latitude, longitude)`
2. Print each city with its coordinates formatted nicely
3. Find and print which city is closest to the equator (lowest absolute latitude)

Use `abs()` for absolute value, `min()` with a `key=` argument to find the closest.
hint:
cities = [("New York", 40.71, -74.01), ...]
min(cities, key=lambda c: abs(c[1]))

codeChecks.message: Loop over your cities to print them.
Use abs() to find the closest to the equator.

successMessage: A real coordinate mapper with immutable data. Tuples protect your coordinates from accidental modification — exactly the right tool. Chapter 9 done!
